section: Psychological, Social, and Biological Foundations of Behavior
provenance: Original content. Concepts calibrated against MCAT QBook Ch3 (Psychology) + Ch6 (Sociology) used privately as ground truth; no source text reproduced.
trademark_notice: MCAT is a registered trademark of the AAMC. This material is independently authored and not endorsed by the AAMC.

passages.title: Detecting a faint tone in background noise
passages.passage_text: A hearing researcher asks volunteers to report whether a faint pure tone is present on each trial. On many trials no tone is played, and on the rest a tone of fixed low intensity is embedded in steady background noise. Participants press one button for 'tone present' and another for 'tone absent.' In a second block, the researcher tells participants that correctly catching a tone will earn a small bonus, without changing the tone's physical intensity. The team records how often listeners correctly report a tone (hits) and how often they report a tone when none was played (false alarms).

questions.stem: The minimum tone intensity a listener can detect on 50% of the trials it is presented is best described as that listener's:
questions.explanation: The absolute threshold is the smallest amount of a stimulus that can be detected on 50% of trials. That is exactly what the described procedure measures for the faint tone.

distractor_notes.B: A difference threshold (just-noticeable difference) is the smallest detectable change between two stimuli, not the smallest detectable single stimulus.
distractor_notes.C: The Weber fraction is the constant proportion by which a stimulus must change to be noticeably different; it concerns discrimination, not absolute detection.
distractor_notes.D: Sensory adaptation is a decline in responsiveness to a constant stimulus over time, not a detection threshold.

questions.ground_truth_ref: absolute threshold definition (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: When the bonus is offered for catching tones, listeners report 'tone present' more often, raising both their hit rate and their false-alarm rate even though the tone intensity is unchanged. According to signal detection theory, the bonus most directly altered the listeners':
questions.explanation: Signal detection theory separates sensitivity from the response criterion. A reward that makes listeners say 'present' more often, increasing both hits and false alarms, reflects a shift toward a more liberal criterion, not a change in the ability to distinguish signal from noise.

distractor_notes.A: Sensitivity (d') reflects how well signal is separated from noise; a criterion shift that raises hits and false alarms together leaves d' essentially unchanged.
distractor_notes.C: The absolute threshold is a property of the sensory system and is not moved by a payoff instruction alone.
distractor_notes.D: Transduction is the conversion of sound energy into neural signals in the cochlea and is not altered by a motivational instruction.

questions.ground_truth_ref: signal detection: criterion vs sensitivity (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: A trial on which a tone is played but the listener reports 'tone absent' is classified in signal detection theory as a:
questions.explanation: When the signal is present but the observer responds 'absent,' the outcome is a miss. The four outcomes are hit (signal present, reports present), miss (present, reports absent), false alarm (absent, reports present), and correct rejection (absent, reports absent).

distractor_notes.A: A hit is a correct 'present' response when the tone is actually present.
distractor_notes.B: A false alarm is a 'present' response when no tone was played.
distractor_notes.D: A correct rejection is an 'absent' response when no tone was played.

questions.ground_truth_ref: signal detection outcome categories (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: The transduction of the tone into neural signals that the listener can ultimately detect begins in which structure?

options.B: The ossicles of the middle ear
options.D: The hair cells of the cochlea

questions.explanation: Transduction of sound into neural impulses occurs when movement of cochlear fluid bends the stereocilia of hair cells in the organ of Corti, opening ion channels and generating receptor potentials.

distractor_notes.A: The tympanic membrane (eardrum) vibrates mechanically but does not convert sound to neural signals.
distractor_notes.B: The ossicles amplify and transmit mechanical vibration to the oval window; they do not perform transduction.
distractor_notes.C: The primary auditory cortex processes signals that have already been transduced and relayed; it is not where transduction occurs.

questions.ground_truth_ref: auditory transduction site (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: The researcher plays a very quiet steady hum continuously in a separate task, and listeners report that after a minute the hum seems to fade even though it continues at the same volume. This best illustrates:
questions.explanation: A constant, unchanging stimulus produces a progressive decline in sensory responsiveness, so it is perceived as weaker over time. This decline is sensory adaptation.

distractor_notes.B: Weber's law describes the proportional relationship needed to detect a difference between stimuli, not the fading of a constant one.
distractor_notes.C: Bottom-up processing is building perception from raw sensory input; it does not explain why a steady stimulus fades.
distractor_notes.D: The just-noticeable difference concerns detecting change between stimuli, not adaptation to a constant one.

questions.ground_truth_ref: sensory adaptation (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

passages.title: Conditioning a startle response in the laboratory
passages.passage_text: In a learning study, a brief puff of air is directed at a participant's eye, reliably producing a protective eyeblink. The experimenter repeatedly sounds a soft chime one second before each air puff. After many pairings, the chime alone comes to produce an eyeblink. In a later phase the chime is presented many times with no air puff, and over trials the eyeblink to the chime gradually disappears. When the participant returns the next day, the chime again briefly elicits a weak eyeblink before fading once more.

questions.stem: Before any training, the air puff produces an eyeblink automatically. In classical conditioning terms, the air puff is the:
questions.explanation: The air puff triggers the eyeblink automatically without prior learning, which is the definition of an unconditioned stimulus.

distractor_notes.A: The conditioned stimulus is the initially neutral cue (the chime) that acquires the ability to elicit a response through pairing.
distractor_notes.C: A conditioned response is a learned reaction to the conditioned stimulus, not the stimulus itself.
distractor_notes.D: A neutral stimulus initially produces no relevant response; the air puff already produces the reflexive blink.

questions.ground_truth_ref: unconditioned stimulus (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: After training, the eyeblink produced by the chime alone is the:
questions.explanation: The eyeblink elicited by the previously neutral chime is a learned reaction to the conditioned stimulus, which defines the conditioned response.

distractor_notes.A: The unconditioned response is the reflexive blink to the air puff itself, present before any learning.
distractor_notes.B: An unconditioned stimulus is a stimulus, not a response, and produces reactions without training.
distractor_notes.D: A discriminative stimulus is an operant-conditioning concept signaling when a behavior will be reinforced, not a classically conditioned reflex.

questions.ground_truth_ref: conditioned response (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: The gradual disappearance of the eyeblink when the chime is presented repeatedly without the air puff is called:
questions.explanation: Presenting the conditioned stimulus repeatedly without the unconditioned stimulus weakens and eventually eliminates the conditioned response. This decline is extinction.

distractor_notes.A: Habituation is a decline in an innate response to a repeated stimulus and does not involve a previously learned CS-US association.
distractor_notes.B: Stimulus generalization is responding to stimuli similar to the conditioned stimulus, not the weakening of the response.
distractor_notes.C: Acquisition is the initial learning phase in which the response is established, the opposite of its disappearance.

questions.ground_truth_ref: extinction in classical conditioning (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: The reappearance of a weak eyeblink to the chime the next day, after extinction, is best explained by:
questions.explanation: After a rest interval following extinction, a previously extinguished conditioned response can briefly reappear when the conditioned stimulus is presented again. This is spontaneous recovery.

distractor_notes.B: Second-order conditioning requires pairing a new neutral stimulus with an already-conditioned stimulus, which did not occur here.
distractor_notes.C: Overshadowing is when a more salient stimulus in a compound blocks conditioning to a weaker one, unrelated to the return of a response after rest.
distractor_notes.D: Latent learning is learning that is not demonstrated until there is an incentive; it is not the recovery of an extinguished reflex.

questions.ground_truth_ref: spontaneous recovery (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: In a follow-up, a chime of a slightly different pitch also elicits a small eyeblink, and the more the pitch resembles the training chime, the larger the eyeblink. This pattern demonstrates:
questions.explanation: Responding to stimuli that resemble the conditioned stimulus, with stronger responses to more similar stimuli, is stimulus generalization.

distractor_notes.A: Discrimination is learning to respond to the trained stimulus but not to different ones, the opposite of the graded responding described.
distractor_notes.C: Extinction is the loss of the conditioned response, not responding to similar stimuli.
distractor_notes.D: Shaping is an operant technique of reinforcing successive approximations of a target behavior, not a classical generalization effect.

questions.ground_truth_ref: stimulus generalization (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

passages.title: Reinforcement schedules in a training program
passages.passage_text: A trainer studies how different consequences shape a target behavior in which a participant presses a lever. In one condition, a food reward is delivered after every tenth press. In another, a reward is delivered after an unpredictable number of presses that averages ten. In a third condition, whenever the participant performs the behavior, an annoying buzzing sound is switched off. The trainer records how quickly each pattern of consequences changes the rate of lever pressing and how the behavior persists when rewards stop.

questions.stem: Delivering a food reward after exactly every tenth press is an example of which reinforcement schedule?
questions.explanation: A reinforcer delivered after a set, unchanging number of responses is a fixed-ratio schedule. Here the reward follows every tenth press.

distractor_notes.A: A variable-ratio schedule reinforces after an unpredictable number of responses, not a fixed count.
distractor_notes.B: A fixed-interval schedule reinforces the first response after a set amount of time has elapsed, not after a set number of responses.
distractor_notes.D: A variable-interval schedule reinforces the first response after an unpredictable time interval, not a response count.

questions.ground_truth_ref: fixed-ratio schedule (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: The condition in which a reward follows an unpredictable number of presses averaging ten is expected to produce:

options.A: The lowest overall response rate of any schedule
options.B: A long pause after each reward
options.C: Responding only near the end of a fixed time period
options.D: A high, steady response rate that is highly resistant to extinction

questions.explanation: Variable-ratio schedules produce high, steady rates of responding and are the most resistant to extinction because the learner cannot predict which response will be reinforced (the pattern underlying gambling).

distractor_notes.A: Ratio schedules generally yield high, not low, response rates; variable-ratio is among the highest.
distractor_notes.B: A long post-reinforcement pause is characteristic of fixed-ratio and fixed-interval schedules, not variable-ratio.
distractor_notes.C: Responding concentrated at the end of a period (a scalloped pattern) is characteristic of fixed-interval schedules.

questions.ground_truth_ref: variable-ratio behavior pattern (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: In the third condition, the behavior increases because performing it switches off an aversive buzzing sound. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Removing an aversive stimulus (the buzzing) to increase a behavior is negative reinforcement: the behavior is strengthened because it takes away something unpleasant.

distractor_notes.B: Positive reinforcement increases behavior by adding a pleasant stimulus, but here something aversive is removed.
distractor_notes.C: Positive punishment adds an aversive stimulus to decrease behavior; here behavior increases.
distractor_notes.D: Negative punishment removes a pleasant stimulus to decrease behavior; here an aversive stimulus is removed and behavior increases.

questions.ground_truth_ref: negative reinforcement (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: To first establish a complex lever-pressing sequence, the trainer rewards behaviors that increasingly resemble the final action, starting with merely approaching the lever. This technique is called:
questions.explanation: Reinforcing successive approximations of a target behavior to build it up gradually is shaping.

distractor_notes.A: Extinction is the weakening of a behavior when reinforcement is withheld, not the building of a new one.
distractor_notes.C: Latent learning is learning that occurs without reinforcement and is only later revealed, not the reinforcement of approximations.
distractor_notes.D: Spontaneous recovery is the reappearance of an extinguished response, unrelated to building a new behavior.

questions.ground_truth_ref: shaping by successive approximations (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: A food reward such as the one used here satisfies a biological need directly. Such a reinforcer is best classified as a:
questions.explanation: A primary reinforcer is innately satisfying because it meets a basic biological need (such as food or water) without prior learning.

distractor_notes.A: A secondary reinforcer gains its reinforcing power through association with a primary reinforcer; food is innately reinforcing.
distractor_notes.B: A conditioned reinforcer is another term for a secondary reinforcer, which food is not.
distractor_notes.D: A discriminative stimulus signals when reinforcement is available; it is not itself the reinforcer.

questions.ground_truth_ref: primary vs secondary reinforcers (QBook Ch3) - original framing

passages.title: How a list of words is remembered
passages.passage_text: Participants hear a list of twenty unrelated words read aloud once and are then asked to recall as many as possible. Researchers note that words from the beginning and end of the list are recalled better than words from the middle. In a second experiment, participants who form a vivid mental image linking each word to a familiar location recall far more words than those who simply repeat the words to themselves. A month later, participants are asked to recognize the original words from a longer list.

questions.stem: The finding that words from the beginning and the end of the list are recalled better than those in the middle is known as the:
questions.explanation: The serial position effect describes superior recall of items at the start (primacy) and end (recency) of a list relative to the middle.

distractor_notes.A: The spacing effect is better retention when study is distributed over time rather than massed, unrelated to list position.
distractor_notes.B: The misinformation effect is the distortion of memory by misleading post-event information, not a position-based recall advantage.
distractor_notes.C: The tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon is a temporary retrieval failure for a known word, not a list-position pattern.

questions.ground_truth_ref: serial position effect (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: Better recall of the last few words on the list, if tested immediately, is usually attributed to those words still being held in:
questions.explanation: The recency portion of the serial position effect reflects the last items still residing in short-term (working) memory at the moment of recall.

distractor_notes.B: The primacy advantage, not recency, is attributed to greater rehearsal into long-term memory.
distractor_notes.C: Procedural memory stores skills and habits, not a just-heard word list.
distractor_notes.D: Iconic memory is a very brief visual sensory store lasting a fraction of a second, not the store for heard words at recall.

questions.ground_truth_ref: recency effect and short-term store (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: Participants who linked each word to a vivid image recalled more words than those who merely repeated them. Their superior encoding is best explained by:
questions.explanation: Connecting new information to meaningful images and existing knowledge is elaborative, deep encoding, which produces stronger, more durable memories than shallow repetition.

distractor_notes.A: Maintenance rehearsal is simple repetition to hold information briefly; it is the weaker strategy the other group used.
distractor_notes.C: Sensory memory decay is the rapid loss of unattended sensory input, not an encoding advantage.
distractor_notes.D: Proactive interference is when old learning disrupts new learning; it impairs, rather than enhances, recall.

questions.ground_truth_ref: levels of processing / elaborative encoding (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: Asking participants to identify the original words within a longer list a month later tests memory primarily through:
questions.explanation: Identifying previously studied items among new ones is a recognition task; the correct answer is presented and the participant must select it.

distractor_notes.A: Free recall requires producing the items without cues, which is not what selecting from a provided list involves.
distractor_notes.B: Relearning measures memory by how much faster material is learned a second time, not by identifying items.
distractor_notes.D: Implicit priming is an unconscious influence of prior exposure on performance, not an explicit recognition test.

questions.ground_truth_ref: recognition vs recall (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: A participant cannot recall a specific word during free recall, but immediately recognizes it on the later recognition test. This dissociation most directly indicates that the word was:

options.B: Lost from sensory memory before storage
options.D: Encoded and stored, but subject to a retrieval failure during recall

questions.explanation: Successful recognition shows the information was encoded and stored; failing to produce it on free recall while recognizing it later indicates a retrieval failure, often relieved by the cues a recognition test provides.

distractor_notes.A: If the word were never encoded, it could not be recognized later.
distractor_notes.B: Loss from sensory memory before storage would prevent later recognition entirely.
distractor_notes.C: Anterograde amnesia is an inability to form new long-term memories; here the memory clearly formed because it was recognized.

questions.ground_truth_ref: encoding/storage/retrieval distinction (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

passages.title: Judgment and problem solving under uncertainty
passages.passage_text: Investigators present volunteers with everyday reasoning problems. In one, participants estimate whether more English words begin with the letter K or have K as their third letter; most say the former, though the latter is actually more common. In another, participants read a description of a quiet, detail-oriented person and judge that the individual is more likely a librarian than a farmer, despite there being far more farmers overall. Participants also try to solve a puzzle that has a known step-by-step method guaranteeing a solution.

questions.stem: Judging words beginning with K as more common because such words come to mind more easily reflects the:
questions.explanation: Estimating frequency by how readily examples come to mind is the availability heuristic. Words that start with K are easier to retrieve than words with K in the third position, biasing the estimate.

distractor_notes.B: The representativeness heuristic judges likelihood by similarity to a prototype, not by ease of retrieval.
distractor_notes.C: Confirmation bias is seeking or weighting evidence that supports one's existing belief, not a frequency estimate based on recall ease.
distractor_notes.D: The framing effect concerns how the wording of options changes choices, not frequency judgments from memory.

questions.ground_truth_ref: availability heuristic (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: Judging the quiet, detail-oriented person to be a librarian rather than a farmer, while ignoring how many more farmers there are, illustrates the representativeness heuristic combined with neglect of:
questions.explanation: The judgment relies on how well the description matches a stereotype (representativeness) while ignoring the base rate, the underlying frequency of farmers versus librarians in the population.

distractor_notes.A: The sunk cost fallacy is continuing an endeavor because of past investment, unrelated to this category judgment.
distractor_notes.C: The fundamental attribution error is over-attributing others' behavior to disposition, not a base-rate neglect in categorization.
distractor_notes.D: Cognitive dissonance is discomfort from holding conflicting beliefs, not a probability judgment error.

questions.ground_truth_ref: representativeness heuristic and base-rate neglect (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: The puzzle that has a known step-by-step method guaranteeing a correct solution is best solved by applying:
questions.explanation: An algorithm is a systematic, step-by-step procedure that guarantees a correct solution if followed, exactly matching the described method.

distractor_notes.A: A heuristic is a mental shortcut that is faster but does not guarantee a correct answer.
distractor_notes.B: Insight is a sudden realization of a solution, not a guaranteed step-by-step procedure.
distractor_notes.D: Trial and error is unsystematic testing of possibilities and does not guarantee an efficient or certain solution.

questions.ground_truth_ref: algorithm vs heuristic (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: A participant insists on using a familiar tool in the customary way and fails to see that it could serve a novel function needed to solve a problem. This obstacle is best described as:
questions.explanation: Functional fixedness is the tendency to perceive objects only in terms of their usual function, which blocks the creative reuse needed to solve the problem.

distractor_notes.A: The availability heuristic is a frequency-judgment shortcut, not an inability to see new uses for an object.
distractor_notes.B: Belief perseverance is clinging to a belief after it has been discredited, not a fixation on an object's usual function.
distractor_notes.C: Divergent thinking is generating many possible solutions, the opposite of the fixation described.

questions.ground_truth_ref: functional fixedness (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

passages.title: Explaining the physiology of emotion
passages.passage_text: A researcher studies how the body and mind combine to produce emotional experience. Participants view a startling film clip while their heart rate and skin conductance are recorded and while they report their felt emotions. The researcher compares several classic theories that disagree about the order in which physiological arousal, cognitive interpretation, and the conscious feeling of an emotion occur. In one variation, participants are injected with a stimulant and placed in a room with either a cheerful or an irritable confederate.

questions.stem: According to the James-Lange theory of emotion, the felt emotion is:

options.A: The perception of bodily physiological changes that follow a stimulus
options.B: A direct result of the brain's appraisal, occurring before any bodily change
options.D: The simultaneous but separate occurrence of arousal and feeling

questions.explanation: The James-Lange theory holds that a stimulus first triggers physiological changes, and the conscious emotion is our perception of those bodily changes (for example, we feel afraid because our heart is pounding).

distractor_notes.B: Appraisal preceding bodily change describes a cognitive-first view, not James-Lange.
distractor_notes.C: James-Lange makes physiological arousal central to emotion, not irrelevant.
distractor_notes.D: Simultaneous, separate arousal and feeling describes the Cannon-Bard theory, not James-Lange.

questions.ground_truth_ref: James-Lange theory (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: The Cannon-Bard theory differs from James-Lange primarily in proposing that:

options.A: Physiological arousal must be cognitively labeled before emotion is felt
options.B: Physiological arousal and the subjective feeling of emotion occur simultaneously and independently
options.C: Emotion always precedes any physiological change
options.D: Emotions are learned entirely through conditioning

questions.explanation: The Cannon-Bard theory proposes that a stimulus triggers physiological arousal and the conscious emotional experience at the same time, as parallel and independent processes rather than one causing the other.

distractor_notes.A: Requiring cognitive labeling of arousal describes the Schachter-Singer two-factor theory, not Cannon-Bard.
distractor_notes.C: Cannon-Bard has arousal and feeling occur together, not feeling first.
distractor_notes.D: Learning emotions through conditioning is a behaviorist idea, not the Cannon-Bard claim.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Cannon-Bard theory (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: In the injection variation, participants who received the stimulant reported feeling happy near the cheerful confederate but angry near the irritable one. This outcome best supports which theory of emotion?
questions.explanation: The Schachter-Singer two-factor theory holds that emotion arises from physiological arousal plus a cognitive label drawn from the situation. Identical arousal interpreted through different social contexts yields different emotions, exactly as observed.

distractor_notes.A: James-Lange predicts a specific emotion from a specific bodily pattern, not the same arousal producing different emotions by context.
distractor_notes.B: Cannon-Bard treats arousal and feeling as independent, so it does not explain why the label depends on the confederate.
distractor_notes.D: Drive-reduction theory concerns motivation to reduce biological needs, not the labeling of arousal as emotion.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Schachter-Singer two-factor theory (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: In a separate task requiring sustained attention, performance is best at a moderate level of arousal and worse when arousal is very low or very high. This relationship is described by:
questions.explanation: The Yerkes-Dodson law describes an inverted-U relationship between arousal and performance: performance peaks at an intermediate level of arousal and declines when arousal is too low or too high.

distractor_notes.A: Drive-reduction theory explains motivation to relieve biological needs, not the arousal-performance curve.
distractor_notes.B: Maslow's hierarchy orders human needs; it does not describe arousal and task performance.
distractor_notes.C: Opponent-process theory explains acquired motivations and after-reactions, not the inverted-U performance relationship.

questions.ground_truth_ref: Yerkes-Dodson law (QBook Ch3) - original framing

passages.title: Becoming a member of society
passages.passage_text: A sociologist follows children and adults through several settings in which they learn the expectations of their society. Young children first learn language, basic values, and acceptable behavior within their household. Later, schools, peer groups, and the media convey additional expectations, sometimes reinforcing and sometimes conflicting with what was learned at home. The study also follows adults entering a strict military training program in which recruits must shed prior habits and adopt an entirely new set of rules and identities.

questions.stem: The household in which children first learn language and basic values is best described as an agent of:
questions.explanation: Primary socialization is the earliest learning of language, norms, and values, occurring mainly within the family during childhood.

distractor_notes.B: Secondary socialization occurs later through institutions such as schools and workplaces, building on the primary stage.
distractor_notes.C: Anticipatory socialization is rehearsing for a future role one expects to occupy, not the first learning in the family.
distractor_notes.D: Resocialization is discarding former behaviors and adopting new ones, typically in adulthood, not initial childhood learning.

questions.ground_truth_ref: primary socialization (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: Schools, peer groups, and the media that convey additional expectations later in life are examples of:
questions.explanation: Agents of socialization are the people, groups, and institutions (family, school, peers, media) that transmit norms, values, and expected behaviors.

distractor_notes.A: Primary reinforcers are innately satisfying stimuli in learning theory, not social institutions.
distractor_notes.C: A total institution is a specific, all-encompassing setting (such as a prison or boot camp); schools and media broadly are not total institutions.
distractor_notes.D: A reference group is a standard for comparison; schools, peers, and media function as socialization agents more broadly than just reference groups.

questions.ground_truth_ref: agents of socialization (QBook Ch6) - original framing

questions.stem: The strict military training program, in which recruits shed prior habits and adopt a new identity under intense supervision, best exemplifies:

options.A: Anticipatory socialization within a peer group

questions.explanation: Boot camp is a total institution that controls all aspects of life and deliberately breaks down former identities to build new ones; this deliberate reshaping is resocialization.

distractor_notes.A: Anticipatory socialization rehearses a future role voluntarily; it does not involve stripping away a prior identity under supervision.
distractor_notes.B: Primary socialization occurs in early childhood within the family, not adult military training.
distractor_notes.D: Cultural diffusion is the spread of cultural traits between societies, unrelated to reshaping individual recruits.

questions.ground_truth_ref: resocialization / total institution (QBook Ch6) - original scenario

questions.stem: A high-school student who begins dressing and speaking like the professionals in a career she hopes to enter is engaging in:
questions.explanation: Anticipatory socialization is learning and rehearsing the norms and behaviors of a role one hopes to occupy in the future, as when the student adopts the style of her intended profession.

distractor_notes.A: Resocialization involves discarding an established identity and adopting a new one, often under institutional pressure, not preparing for an aspirational role.
distractor_notes.B: Reverse socialization is when younger people teach norms (e.g., technology) to older generations, not preparing for a future career.
distractor_notes.C: A degradation ceremony strips a person of identity; it is not voluntary preparation for a desired role.

questions.ground_truth_ref: anticipatory socialization (QBook Ch6) - original scenario

passages.title: Types of social groups and networks
passages.passage_text: A researcher maps the social ties of employees at a large firm. Some employees belong to small, close-knit friendship circles marked by deep, enduring emotional bonds, while others interact mainly through large, impersonal project teams organized around specific goals. The researcher also examines how information travels: some individuals connect otherwise separate clusters of coworkers, while others are embedded entirely within a single tight cluster. Finally, the study notes how employees describe their own department versus rival departments.

questions.stem: The small, close-knit friendship circles marked by deep, enduring emotional bonds are best classified as:
questions.explanation: A primary group is small and characterized by close, intimate, enduring relationships, exactly matching the described friendship circles.

distractor_notes.B: A secondary group is larger, more impersonal, and goal-oriented, describing the project teams rather than the friendship circles.
distractor_notes.C: An out-group is a group one does not identify with, a matter of identity rather than intimacy of bonds.
distractor_notes.D: An aggregate is merely people in the same place at the same time with no ongoing relationships.

questions.ground_truth_ref: primary group (QBook Ch6) - original scenario

questions.stem: The large, impersonal project teams organized around specific shared goals are best described as:
questions.explanation: Secondary groups are larger, more formal and impersonal, and organized around a specific purpose or task rather than intimate bonds, which fits the goal-oriented project teams.

distractor_notes.A: Primary groups are small and intimate, the opposite of large impersonal teams.
distractor_notes.C: A reference group is one used as a standard for self-evaluation, a role that is defined by comparison rather than by size and formality.
distractor_notes.D: An in-group is one a person identifies with; that concerns identification, not the formal, task-oriented structure described.

questions.ground_truth_ref: secondary group (QBook Ch6) - original scenario

questions.stem: Employees who connect otherwise separate clusters of coworkers occupy network positions that provide:

options.A: Strong ties that duplicate existing information
options.C: Weak ties that bridge groups and provide access to non-redundant information

questions.explanation: Individuals who link separate clusters typically do so through weak ties, which bridge otherwise disconnected groups and channel novel, non-redundant information between them (the strength of weak ties).

distractor_notes.A: Strong ties within a tight cluster tend to circulate the same information; they do not bridge separate clusters.
distractor_notes.B: Bridging positions are influential in information flow, not irrelevant to it.
distractor_notes.D: A total institution is an all-controlling residential setting, unrelated to a bridging network position.

questions.ground_truth_ref: social networks / strength of weak ties (QBook Ch6) - original scenario

questions.stem: Employees consistently describe their own department in favorable terms while disparaging rival departments. The rival department functions, for these employees, as an:
questions.explanation: An out-group is a group toward which one feels a sense of separation or opposition. Favoring one's own department while disparaging rivals treats the rivals as an out-group.

distractor_notes.A: The in-group is the employees' own department, which they favor, not the rival they disparage.
distractor_notes.B: An aggregate is people sharing a place without social ties; a rival department is an organized group.
distractor_notes.C: A dyad is a two-person group, which does not describe an entire rival department.

questions.ground_truth_ref: in-group vs out-group (QBook Ch6) - original scenario

passages.title: Elements of culture in a community study
passages.passage_text: An anthropologist documents the culture of a coastal community. She records the tools, boats, and buildings the community uses, as well as its shared beliefs about right and wrong, its language, and its customs. Some rules are strictly enforced with severe penalties, while others are minor everyday etiquette whose violation draws only mild disapproval. She also notes how children learn these expectations from elders and how contact with a neighboring community has introduced new practices.

questions.stem: The tools, boats, and buildings the anthropologist records are best classified as part of the community's:
questions.explanation: Material culture consists of the physical objects a society creates and uses, such as tools, vessels, and buildings.

distractor_notes.B: Nonmaterial culture consists of intangible elements such as beliefs, values, norms, and language, not physical objects.
distractor_notes.C: Values are abstract standards of what is desirable, part of nonmaterial culture, not physical artifacts.
distractor_notes.D: Sanctions are rewards or penalties that enforce norms, not physical cultural objects.

questions.ground_truth_ref: material culture (QBook Ch6) - original scenario

questions.stem: Strictly enforced rules whose violation brings severe penalties, and which a society considers morally significant, are best described as:
questions.explanation: Mores are norms tied to a society's moral standards; their violation is treated as seriously wrong and draws strong sanctions.

distractor_notes.A: Folkways are everyday customs and etiquette whose violation brings only mild disapproval, not severe penalties.
distractor_notes.C: Material culture refers to physical objects, not enforced norms.
distractor_notes.D: Symbols are things that carry shared meaning; they are not the same as strongly enforced moral norms.

questions.ground_truth_ref: mores vs folkways (QBook Ch6) - original scenario

questions.stem: The minor everyday etiquette whose violation draws only mild disapproval is best classified as:
questions.explanation: Folkways are informal norms of everyday etiquette and custom; violating them is considered impolite rather than immoral and draws only mild disapproval.

distractor_notes.A: Mores are morally weighty norms whose violation brings strong sanctions, not mild disapproval.
distractor_notes.B: A taboo is an especially strong prohibition against something regarded as utterly forbidden, far stronger than everyday etiquette.
distractor_notes.D: Laws are formally codified norms enforced by the state, not informal etiquette.

questions.ground_truth_ref: folkways (QBook Ch6) - original scenario

questions.stem: The introduction of new practices into the community through contact with a neighboring community is an example of:
questions.explanation: Cultural diffusion is the spread of cultural elements, such as practices or technologies, from one society or group to another through contact.

distractor_notes.A: Cultural relativism is the principle of judging a culture by its own standards, not the transfer of practices.
distractor_notes.B: Ethnocentrism is judging other cultures by one's own standards, not the spread of a practice.
distractor_notes.C: Assimilation is a minority group adopting the dominant culture; the passage describes new practices arriving, not one community being absorbed.

questions.ground_truth_ref: cultural diffusion (QBook Ch6) - original scenario

passages.title: The biological substrate of behavior
passages.passage_text: A neuroscientist studies how brain structures and chemical signaling underlie behavior. She records the activity of individual neurons as they communicate across synapses, and she maps which regions of the brain become active during specific tasks such as balance, memory formation, and the regulation of fear. She also examines how drugs that alter specific neurotransmitters change mood and movement in patients.

questions.stem: When a neuron fires, a chemical messenger is released into the synaptic cleft to communicate with the next neuron. This chemical messenger is a:
questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are the chemical messengers released from the presynaptic terminal into the synaptic cleft, where they bind receptors on the postsynaptic neuron to transmit the signal.

distractor_notes.B: Hormones are chemical messengers carried through the bloodstream by the endocrine system, not released across a synaptic cleft.
distractor_notes.C: A second messenger relays signals inside the cell after a receptor is activated; it does not cross the synapse.
distractor_notes.D: The myelin sheath is an insulating layer around axons that speeds conduction; it is not a chemical messenger.

questions.ground_truth_ref: neurotransmitter definition (QBook Ch3) - original framing

questions.stem: A patient with a movement disorder is found to have degeneration of dopamine-producing neurons in a brain region controlling movement. Loss of which neurotransmitter is most directly implicated?
questions.explanation: The scenario specifies degeneration of dopamine-producing neurons in a motor region; loss of dopamine (as in Parkinson-type movement disorders) is therefore the directly implicated deficit.

distractor_notes.A: Serotonin is chiefly involved in mood, sleep, and appetite rather than this dopaminergic motor pathway.
distractor_notes.C: Acetylcholine contributes to muscle activation and memory, but the described deficit is specifically in dopamine neurons.
distractor_notes.D: GABA is the main inhibitory neurotransmitter; it is not the messenger lost when dopamine neurons degenerate.

questions.ground_truth_ref: dopamine and movement (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: During the task requiring balance and coordination of movement, which brain structure would the researcher expect to be especially active?
questions.explanation: The cerebellum coordinates voluntary movement, balance, and posture, so it is expected to be highly engaged during a balance-and-coordination task.

distractor_notes.A: The hippocampus is central to forming new memories, not to balance and coordination.
distractor_notes.B: The amygdala processes emotions such as fear, not motor coordination.
distractor_notes.D: The medulla oblongata controls autonomic functions such as breathing and heart rate, not fine balance and coordination.

questions.ground_truth_ref: cerebellum function (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

questions.stem: The researcher observes that a small almond-shaped structure becomes highly active when participants view threatening images and experience fear. This structure is the:
questions.explanation: The amygdala is central to processing fear and other emotionally salient stimuli, so it is the structure activated when viewing threatening images.

distractor_notes.A: The hypothalamus regulates homeostatic drives such as hunger, thirst, and temperature and coordinates the endocrine response, rather than serving as the primary fear-detection structure.
distractor_notes.B: The thalamus is a relay station that routes sensory information to the cortex; it is not the primary center for fear.
distractor_notes.C: The corpus callosum is the fiber bundle connecting the two cerebral hemispheres, not an emotion-processing center.

questions.ground_truth_ref: amygdala and fear (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

discrete_questions.stem: According to Weber's law, the amount by which a stimulus must change to be just noticeably different is:

options.A: A constant proportion of the original stimulus intensity
options.B: A fixed absolute quantity regardless of the starting intensity
options.C: Determined only by the observer's response criterion

discrete_questions.explanation: Weber's law states that the just-noticeable difference is a constant fraction (the Weber fraction) of the original stimulus magnitude, so more intense stimuli require larger absolute changes to be detected.

distractor_notes.B: The change is a constant proportion, not a fixed absolute amount, of the starting intensity.
distractor_notes.C: The response criterion is a signal-detection concept about decision bias, not the basis of Weber's law.
distractor_notes.D: The required change depends directly on the original stimulus, since it is a proportion of it.

discrete_questions.stem: A person perceives a complete circle even though the drawing consists of a dashed line with small gaps. This tendency to perceive incomplete figures as whole reflects the Gestalt principle of:
discrete_questions.explanation: Closure is the Gestalt principle by which we mentally fill in gaps to perceive a complete, whole figure, such as seeing a full circle from a broken outline.

distractor_notes.A: Proximity is grouping elements that are near one another, not completing a gapped figure.
distractor_notes.C: Similarity is grouping elements that look alike, not filling in missing parts.
distractor_notes.D: Continuity is perceiving smooth, continuous paths rather than abrupt changes, not completing an incomplete shape.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: Gestalt principle of closure (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

discrete_questions.stem: A dog learns to salivate to a bell but not to a buzzer, because only the bell has been paired with food. The dog's ability to respond differently to the two sounds demonstrates:
discrete_questions.explanation: Responding to one conditioned stimulus but not to a similar one that has never been paired with the unconditioned stimulus is stimulus discrimination.

distractor_notes.A: Generalization would mean responding similarly to both the bell and the buzzer, the opposite of discriminating between them.
distractor_notes.B: Extinction is the loss of a conditioned response when the CS is no longer paired with the US, not distinguishing between stimuli.
distractor_notes.D: Habituation is a decline in an innate response to a repeated stimulus, not a learned CS-US discrimination.

discrete_questions.stem: A commuter buys a lottery-style scratch ticket each day because occasionally, at unpredictable intervals of days, one ticket wins a small prize. The persistence of this daily buying behavior is best explained by reinforcement on a:
discrete_questions.explanation: A reward that becomes available after unpredictable amounts of time, reinforcing the first response after that interval, defines a variable-interval schedule, which produces steady, persistent responding.

distractor_notes.A: A fixed-interval schedule reinforces after a set, predictable time period, but here the winning intervals are unpredictable.
distractor_notes.B: A fixed-ratio schedule reinforces after a set number of responses, not after variable time periods.
distractor_notes.C: Continuous reinforcement rewards every response; here only occasional tickets win.

discrete_questions.stem: A rat that has freely explored a maze without any reward later runs it quickly for food, revealing knowledge it had already acquired. This best illustrates:
discrete_questions.explanation: Latent learning is learning that occurs without reinforcement and is not demonstrated in performance until there is an incentive to do so, as when the rat later uses its cognitive map for food.

distractor_notes.B: Insight learning is the sudden grasp of a solution to a problem, not previously acquired knowledge revealed once rewarded.
distractor_notes.C: Observational learning is acquiring behavior by watching a model, but the rat learned through its own exploration.
distractor_notes.D: Classical conditioning involves associating stimuli to elicit reflexive responses, not forming a spatial map through exploration.

discrete_questions.stem: Memory of how to ride a bicycle, which is expressed through performance rather than conscious verbal recollection, is an example of:
discrete_questions.explanation: Procedural memory is a type of implicit, nondeclarative memory for skills and actions that is expressed through doing rather than conscious recall, such as riding a bicycle.

distractor_notes.A: Episodic memory is the conscious recollection of specific personal events, not a motor skill.
distractor_notes.C: Semantic memory is conscious knowledge of facts and concepts, not a performed skill.
distractor_notes.D: Working memory temporarily holds and manipulates information in the moment, not a long-term motor skill.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: procedural vs declarative memory (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

discrete_questions.stem: After learning a new phone number, a person struggles to recall an old number they had used for years. When new learning interferes with the retrieval of previously stored information, the effect is called:
discrete_questions.explanation: Retroactive interference occurs when newly learned information disrupts the retrieval of older memories, as when the new number interferes with recalling the old one.

distractor_notes.A: Proactive interference is when old learning disrupts new learning, the reverse of what is described.
distractor_notes.B: Source amnesia is attributing a memory to the wrong source, not interference between two numbers.
distractor_notes.D: Encoding failure means the information was never adequately stored; here the old number was learned but is now hard to retrieve.

discrete_questions.stem: A child says 'I goed to the store,' applying a regular past-tense rule to an irregular verb. This overgeneralization of a grammatical rule is best described as:
discrete_questions.explanation: Overregularization is applying a regular grammatical rule (adding -ed) to an irregular word, a common and revealing stage of language development that shows the child has internalized the rule.

distractor_notes.A: Telegraphic speech is early two-word utterances that omit function words, not misapplied grammatical rules.
distractor_notes.B: Fast mapping is rapidly learning the meaning of a new word after minimal exposure, not applying a grammar rule.
distractor_notes.C: A phonemic error involves mispronouncing speech sounds, not misapplying a grammatical rule.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: overregularization in language (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

discrete_questions.stem: An investor keeps pouring money into a failing venture because of the large amount already invested, rather than judging the venture on its future prospects. This reasoning error is the:
discrete_questions.explanation: The sunk cost fallacy is continuing to invest in a losing course of action because of resources already committed, even though those past costs cannot be recovered and should not drive future decisions.

distractor_notes.B: The availability heuristic estimates likelihood by ease of recall, not persistence due to prior investment.
distractor_notes.C: Anchoring bias is over-relying on an initial reference value in judgments, not committing further because of past costs.
distractor_notes.D: Confirmation bias is favoring information that supports existing beliefs, not the influence of prior investment.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: sunk cost fallacy (QBook Ch3) - original scenario

discrete_questions.stem: According to drive-reduction theory, motivation to eat arises primarily from:

options.A: A pull from external incentives independent of internal state
options.B: An internal state of tension (a drive) created by a physiological need, which behavior acts to reduce
options.C: A desire for optimal cognitive challenge

discrete_questions.explanation: Drive-reduction theory holds that a physiological need (such as lack of food) creates an aroused state of tension called a drive, and the organism is motivated to behave in ways that reduce that drive and restore homeostasis.

distractor_notes.A: A pull from external incentives describes incentive theory, which complements but is distinct from drive-reduction's internal-need emphasis.
distractor_notes.C: Seeking optimal challenge reflects arousal or optimal-level theory, not drive reduction toward homeostasis.
distractor_notes.D: Cognitive labeling of arousal is part of the Schachter-Singer theory of emotion, not drive-reduction motivation.

discrete_questions.stem: In Maslow's hierarchy of needs, which needs must generally be reasonably satisfied before a person is strongly motivated to pursue self-actualization?

options.C: The lower needs, including physiological, safety, love/belonging, and esteem needs
options.D: No other needs; self-actualization comes first

discrete_questions.explanation: Maslow's hierarchy is typically arranged so that lower-level needs (physiological, safety, love/belonging, and esteem) are largely satisfied before the individual is strongly motivated toward self-actualization at the top.

distractor_notes.A: Physiological needs are only the base; safety, belonging, and esteem needs also generally precede self-actualization.
distractor_notes.B: Esteem is just below self-actualization, but the needs beneath esteem must also be reasonably met.
distractor_notes.D: Self-actualization is the highest level and is pursued after lower needs are met, not first.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: Maslow's hierarchy of needs (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: A sociologist studies how a hospital, as a formal organization with codified rules, ranked positions, and impersonal procedures, coordinates the work of thousands of employees. This form of organization is best described as a:
discrete_questions.explanation: A bureaucracy is a formal organization characterized by explicit rules, a hierarchy of ranked positions, division of labor, and impersonal procedures, which fits the described hospital administration.

distractor_notes.A: A primary group is small and intimate, not a large formally structured organization.
distractor_notes.B: A subculture is a group with distinct cultural patterns within a larger culture, not a formal administrative structure.
distractor_notes.C: A dyad is a two-person group, far smaller than the described organization.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: bureaucracy as formal organization (QBook Ch6) - original scenario

discrete_questions.stem: Public schooling teaches not only academic content but also punctuality, respect for authority, and standing in line, which prepares children to function in society. Sociologists refer to these unofficial lessons as part of the:
discrete_questions.explanation: The hidden curriculum refers to the implicit lessons in norms, values, and behavior (such as obedience, punctuality, and order) that schools transmit alongside the official academic content.

distractor_notes.B: The formal curriculum is the explicit academic content, not the implicit behavioral lessons described.
distractor_notes.C: A degradation ceremony strips a person of identity; it is not a routine feature of everyday schooling.
distractor_notes.D: Anticipatory socialization is rehearsing a future role; here the children are being socialized, and the concept named is the hidden curriculum.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: hidden curriculum in education (QBook Ch6) - original scenario

discrete_questions.stem: A society transmits religious beliefs and practices from one generation to the next through teaching and shared ritual. This intergenerational passing on of a society's culture is best described as:
discrete_questions.explanation: Cultural transmission is the process by which a society passes its culture, including beliefs, practices, and values, from one generation to the next through socialization.

distractor_notes.A: Cultural lag is the delay between changes in material culture and adjustments in nonmaterial culture, not the passing on of culture across generations.
distractor_notes.C: A counterculture actively rejects the dominant culture; this describes transmission, not resistance.
distractor_notes.D: Assimilation is a group adopting another culture and losing its own, not passing culture to the next generation.

discrete_questions.stem: A sociologist argues that the family unit persists across societies because it performs essential tasks such as raising and socializing children and providing emotional support. This explanation of a social institution by the needs it meets for society reflects which theoretical perspective?
discrete_questions.explanation: Structural functionalism explains social institutions in terms of the functions they perform to maintain social stability, such as the family socializing children and providing support.

distractor_notes.A: Conflict theory emphasizes how institutions perpetuate inequality and power struggles, not the shared functions they serve.
distractor_notes.B: Symbolic interactionism focuses on meaning created through everyday interaction, not society-wide functions of institutions.
distractor_notes.D: Rational choice theory explains behavior through individual cost-benefit calculation, not the functional needs of society as a whole.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: structural functionalism and the family (QBook Ch6) - original framing

free_response_questions.prompt: Distinguish classical conditioning from operant conditioning, naming the kind of behavior each governs and the key relationship the learner acquires.

rubric.description: Classical: involuntary/reflexive response; associating two stimuli (CS with US).
required_concepts: classical conditioning involves involuntary/reflexive responses
associates a neutral stimulus with an unconditioned stimulus
disqualifiers: says classical conditioning shapes voluntary behavior via consequences
conflates it with operant conditioning

rubric.description: Operant: voluntary behavior changed by its consequences (reinforcement/punishment).
required_concepts: operant conditioning involves voluntary behavior
behavior changed by consequences (reinforcement/punishment)
disqualifiers: says operant conditioning pairs two stimuli
says operant conditioning acts on reflexes

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Classical conditioning shapes involuntary, reflexive responses by associating a neutral stimulus with an unconditioned stimulus. Operant conditioning changes voluntary behavior according to its consequences (reinforcement or punishment).

free_response_questions.prompt: Distinguish negative reinforcement from punishment, and state the effect each has on the frequency of the target behavior.

rubric.description: Negative reinforcement removes an aversive stimulus and increases the behavior.
required_concepts: negative reinforcement removes/avoids an aversive stimulus
increases the behavior
disqualifiers: says negative reinforcement decreases behavior
equates negative reinforcement with punishment

rubric.description: Punishment decreases the behavior.
required_concepts: punishment decreases/suppresses the behavior
disqualifiers: says punishment increases behavior
says punishment and reinforcement have the same effect

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Negative reinforcement removes an aversive stimulus, which increases the target behavior; it is not punishment. Punishment (positive or negative) decreases the behavior.

free_response_questions.prompt: Describe the three-stage (Atkinson-Shiffrin) model of memory, naming the stages in order and one feature that distinguishes short-term from long-term memory.

rubric.description: Names sensory memory → short-term (working) memory → long-term memory, in order.
required_concepts: sensory memory
short-term/working memory
long-term memory
in that order
disqualifiers: puts the stages out of order
omits sensory memory entirely

rubric.description: Contrasts limited/brief short-term with large/durable long-term (capacity or duration).
required_concepts: short-term is limited capacity / brief duration
long-term is large capacity / durable
disqualifiers: says short-term memory is unlimited and permanent
says long-term memory holds only a few items for seconds

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Information flows sensory memory → short-term (working) memory → long-term memory. Short-term memory holds a limited amount (~7 items) briefly, whereas long-term memory has essentially unlimited capacity and long duration.

free_response_questions.prompt: Explain the difference between proactive and retroactive interference in forgetting, giving the direction of interference in each.

rubric.description: Proactive: old learning interferes with new memories.
required_concepts: proactive interference: old/prior learning disrupts new learning
disqualifiers: says proactive interference is new disrupting old
reverses the direction

rubric.description: Retroactive: new learning interferes with old memories.
required_concepts: retroactive interference: new learning disrupts old/previous memories
disqualifiers: says retroactive interference is old disrupting new
conflates the two

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Proactive interference is when previously learned information disrupts recall of newly learned material; retroactive interference is when new learning disrupts recall of older material.

free_response_questions.prompt: Define the absolute threshold in sensation, and explain how signal detection theory adds to this idea by accounting for factors beyond stimulus intensity.

rubric.description: Absolute threshold = minimum stimulus intensity detectable a set fraction (≈50%) of the time.
required_concepts: minimum intensity detectable ~50% of the time
smallest detectable stimulus
disqualifiers: says absolute threshold is the smallest detectable difference between stimuli (that is difference threshold)

rubric.description: Signal detection theory adds non-sensory factors (expectation/motivation, response bias, noise).
required_concepts: signal detection accounts for decision/response bias, expectations, or noise
detection is not purely about intensity
disqualifiers: says detection depends only on stimulus intensity
ignores observer/decision factors

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The absolute threshold is the minimum stimulus intensity a person can detect about 50% of the time. Signal detection theory adds that detection also depends on non-sensory factors—expectations, motivation, response bias, and background noise—not intensity alone.

free_response_questions.prompt: Contrast the availability heuristic with the representativeness heuristic, giving what each judgment is based on.

rubric.description: Availability: judging likelihood by how easily examples come to mind.
required_concepts: availability heuristic: ease of recalling examples
how readily instances come to mind
disqualifiers: defines availability as matching a prototype
swaps the two heuristics

rubric.description: Representativeness: judging by similarity to a prototype/stereotype.
required_concepts: representativeness heuristic: similarity to a prototype/stereotype/category
disqualifiers: defines representativeness as ease of recall
ignores prototype matching

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The availability heuristic judges probability by how easily instances come to mind, while the representativeness heuristic judges by how closely something matches a prototype or stereotype of a category.

free_response_questions.prompt: In Piaget's theory, distinguish assimilation from accommodation, and give the direction of change to the child's schema in each.

rubric.description: Assimilation: fitting new information into an existing schema (schema unchanged).
required_concepts: assimilation fits new info into an existing schema
schema is not changed
disqualifiers: says assimilation changes the schema
swaps the two terms

rubric.description: Accommodation: changing/creating a schema to fit new information.
required_concepts: accommodation modifies or creates a schema
schema changes to fit new information
disqualifiers: says accommodation leaves the schema unchanged
conflates it with assimilation

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Assimilation incorporates new information into an existing schema without changing it; accommodation modifies or creates a schema to fit information that doesn't fit the old one.

free_response_questions.prompt: Contrast the James-Lange and Cannon-Bard theories of emotion with respect to the order/timing of physiological arousal and the conscious feeling of emotion.

rubric.description: James-Lange: physiological arousal comes first and the emotion is the interpretation of it.
required_concepts: James-Lange: bodily arousal precedes/causes the emotion
emotion is the perception of the physiological response
disqualifiers: says James-Lange has emotion causing arousal
swaps the theories

rubric.description: Cannon-Bard: arousal and the emotional experience occur simultaneously/independently.
required_concepts: Cannon-Bard: arousal and emotion occur at the same time
simultaneous and independent
disqualifiers: says Cannon-Bard has arousal strictly before emotion
says Cannon-Bard requires cognitive labeling first

free_response_questions.reference_answer: James-Lange says physiological arousal occurs first and the felt emotion is our interpretation of that bodily state. Cannon-Bard says the arousal and the conscious emotion happen simultaneously and independently.

free_response_questions.prompt: Distinguish material culture from nonmaterial culture in sociology, giving an example of each.

rubric.description: Material culture = physical objects/artifacts of a group (with an example).
required_concepts: material culture is physical objects/artifacts
example such as tools, buildings, clothing
disqualifiers: says material culture is beliefs/values
gives a nonmaterial example for material culture

rubric.description: Nonmaterial culture = ideas/beliefs/values/norms (with an example).
required_concepts: nonmaterial culture is ideas, beliefs, values, or norms
example such as language, customs, laws
disqualifiers: says nonmaterial culture is physical objects
gives a physical example for nonmaterial culture

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Material culture is the physical objects a group creates and uses (tools, buildings, clothing). Nonmaterial culture is its intangible ideas—beliefs, values, norms, and language.

free_response_questions.prompt: Distinguish a primary group from a secondary group in sociology, giving the nature of the relationships in each.

rubric.description: Primary group: small, close, enduring, personal relationships (e.g. family).
required_concepts: primary group has close/intimate, enduring, personal ties
example such as family or close friends
disqualifiers: says primary groups are large and impersonal
swaps the definitions

rubric.description: Secondary group: larger, impersonal, goal-/task-oriented relationships.
required_concepts: secondary group is impersonal / task- or goal-oriented
example such as coworkers or a class
disqualifiers: says secondary groups are intimate and lifelong
conflates it with a primary group

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Primary groups are small and marked by close, enduring, personal relationships (e.g. family). Secondary groups are larger and impersonal, organized around a shared task or goal (e.g. coworkers).

free_response_questions.prompt: Define socialization and name two agents of socialization, explaining what each contributes.

rubric.description: Defines socialization as learning a society's norms, values, and behaviors.
required_concepts: socialization = learning the norms/values/roles of society
process of internalizing culture
disqualifiers: defines socialization as being social/extroverted
says it is genetic transmission of behavior

rubric.description: Names two valid agents (e.g. family, school, peers, media) with a role for each.
required_concepts: names two agents such as family, school, peers, or media
gives what each contributes
disqualifiers: names only one agent
names non-agents (e.g. weather) as agents of socialization

free_response_questions.reference_answer: Socialization is the lifelong process of learning and internalizing a society's norms, values, and roles. Agents include the family (earliest values and language), school (rules and knowledge), peers, and media—each transmitting cultural expectations.

free_response_questions.prompt: Using the functionalist distinction, give one manifest function and one latent function of a social institution such as schooling, and explain the difference.

rubric.description: Manifest function = intended, recognized consequence (e.g. schooling teaches skills).
required_concepts: manifest function is intended/recognized
example such as education transmitting knowledge/skills
disqualifiers: defines manifest function as unintended
swaps manifest and latent

rubric.description: Latent function = unintended/hidden consequence (e.g. schooling as childcare or social networking).
required_concepts: latent function is unintended/unrecognized
example such as childcare, friendship networks, dating
disqualifiers: defines latent function as the intended goal
conflates it with a dysfunction only

free_response_questions.reference_answer: A manifest function is an institution's intended, recognized consequence—e.g. schools teach academic skills. A latent function is an unintended, hidden consequence—e.g. schools also provide childcare and social networks.

free_response_questions.prompt: During a frightening event the body mobilizes for 'fight or flight.' Name the branch of the autonomic nervous system responsible and describe two of its physiological effects.

rubric.description: Identifies the sympathetic nervous system.
required_concepts: sympathetic nervous system
sympathetic branch of the autonomic nervous system
disqualifiers: says the parasympathetic system drives fight-or-flight
names the somatic nervous system

rubric.description: Gives two correct sympathetic effects (e.g. ↑ heart rate, pupil dilation, ↓ digestion).
required_concepts: two effects such as increased heart rate, pupil dilation, bronchodilation, decreased digestion
disqualifiers: lists parasympathetic 'rest and digest' effects
gives only one effect

free_response_questions.reference_answer: The sympathetic nervous system drives fight-or-flight. Its effects include increased heart rate and blood pressure, pupil dilation, bronchodilation, and decreased digestive activity—preparing the body for action.
